headlines.title: FIGHT MADE ON PAVING
headlines.subtitle: Hemphill Street Work May Be Stopped Because Water Mains Are Not Laid.

headlines.title: BELT ROAD IS LOSER IN 2 DAMAGE SUITS
headlines.subtitle: $25,000 Verdict Given Result of Auto Accident of January 10.
headlines.fullText: Quick Judgment for damages was given Tuesday in two suits against the Fort Worth Belt Railway Company re- sulting from the automobile accident of Jan. 10, 1914, in which four persons were killed. The judgment in each of the two suits was for $12,500. The cases were heard before Judge Buck without Maggie B. MeLemore, widow of E. E. Mclemore, one of the men killed. Bet her four ony phHaren " generelt children. E. F Mclemore Jr .. Lemore and John D. Hebern she was joined in the sutt by Robert H. En Caroline Member H E. Mclemore. The widow was award- ed 87,500 and the children $1,250 each. Judgment was rendered for the de- fendant company against the plaintiffs. Robert H. and Caroline Mclemore. Ethel Kelly Corley, widow of Eugene time, also asked $25,000 on behalf of herself and her daughter. Gwendolyn Netmoms, Corley. In the suit by Mra. John H. Corles. mother of age Que decided in favor of the widow and child, but refused to amount of the judgment, $7.500 is to go to the widow and $5,000 to the child.

headlines.title: PUBLIC HEARING ON MILK LAW SEPTEMBER 9
headlines.subtitle: People to Express Views on Measure Proposed by Health Board.
headlines.fullText: The ultimate consumer will be let in on a public conference on the pro- posed new milk ordinance at city hall Sept. 9 at 9 a. m. He may then express his views to the city commis- sinn. This was announced at the commis- Tion meeting Tuesday following speeches by Claude McCaleb and Lloyd Burns, attorneys for the dairymen; Dr. M. headlines.title: MEMORIAL SUNDAY FOR LATE BISHOPS
headlines.subtitle: Late Territorial Head of M. E. Church Was Well Known in Fort Worth,
headlines.fullText: Memorial will be held Sun- day at St. Paul's Church for Bishop W LE. Wehopen fre DD .. announced Monday. He the residential bishop he was known as For two years he had offiela Hendene in Oklahoma City, from which place He supervised the work of the church In the Southwest. His career had been remarkable. For ah was a bricklayer, and until his was said that he laid all the brick of the first church built under his pastor- He had visited In Fort Worth sev AKO. when he spoke at St. Paul's church. He lectured at the city hall in Fort Worth also. His most famous lee. - "Buttoned Up People." The residence at Oklahoma City can- not be filled until the general conter ence of 1916. The work of this dis- triet in the meantime will have to be borne by the other bishops of the church from their present residences,

headlines.title: LATTIMORE MEASURE IS FAVORED
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram AUSTIN, Sept. 1 Warehouse bills closed doors this morning after the body had been resolved Into a com- mittee of the whole with Senator Car- ter as chairman. Before outsiders were excluded the Lattimore bill, which provides for In- arianaowneramp Orders and state state weighers and braden supervision was reported favoritos .. perSetor weg serted he would rather go home with- out passing ny erice the Democrats principle of individual ownership. Senator Cowell also opposed any form of state or mu- nicipal ownership. Senator Westbrook spoke for state ownership and control. Senator MeNealus asked investigation of differences between Liverpool prices and Texas prices. Senator Conner had adopted an amendment to the Wiley warehouse bill placing the waren on the commis- sioner of Insurance and banking. Senator MeNealus had adopted an by providing that any building, cov- sidered a warehouse. That section of the Wiley bill which provides that gin- ners must provide warehouse facilities, was stricken out. In the house the administration bill with the amendments providing for state control and leasing of ware- houses was debated. After reading of the bill Humphrey offered un feature ment to the state owndusion. which precipitated the discussion. McAskill spoke against state owner- ship and control and sales bill for state operation of-ame pos means the building of a sisante po- atacar mammas and banking the mener to appoint the managers. Spanker Terrell read a letter from James E. Ferguson declining to address the special session. Ferguson says that the purposes for which It is in session are too important to permit of any diversion from the business in hand.

headlines.title: MINE LEADERS FOUND GUILTY OF CONTEMPT
headlines.subtitle: Seven Persons, Including Peter R. Stewart, Given Jail Sentences.
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. LITTLE ROCK. Ark., Sept. 1 .- Seven members of the United Mine Workers of America, including Peter R. Stew- art, who recently resigned as Press dent of the district union composed of Arkansas, Oklahoma and Texas, to- day were found guilty of contempt of court by Fedena dashed to serve from thirty days to four months in Jail. The charges grew out of recent riots at the coal mean the men sentenced to four monde charged with being wetested in the riots which occurred -Her the operators had obatined an in- junction.

headlines.title: BIG PLANS FOR FAT-LEAN GAME
headlines.subtitle: Parade Will Form at 2:30, Sept. 11 to Go to Park for Fight to Finish.
headlines.fullText: Detaile of plans for the Rotary Club field day and baseball game between the Fats and the Leans, to be held at Morris Park Sept. 11 for the benefit of Cumberland Rest, were outlined to the general committee Tuesday at a luncheon at the Westbrook hotel which started at laals p. m. The committee on tickets has had a,bog primicd and expect to dispose of every one of them. Plans for a parade have been made composed of L. P. Robertson, C. B. Brown, J. R. Overstreet, W. W. Henitel and Homer B. Lyne, The parade will consist of a truck carrying the band. oneste ute one carrying the members of the Fat team, a truck-or buggy-carrying the members of the Lean toam, automobiles carrying the members of the Rotary Club and their wives and the women completas the board of the Cumberland Rest. The parade will form at 2:30 p. m. In front of the Majestic theater and en attente cific union station, turning north on Stain street there and going straight on to Morris Park The general committee in charge of Chipps, chairman: A. B. Pace, Ben F. Keith, W. G. Turner and W. N. Moore.
